children.title: Creating DataFrames and Understanding Structures
children.summary: Different methods for creating DataFrames were discussed, including setting and resetting indices. Emphasis was placed on naming columns for clarity, with an example of a DataFrame containing a single column 'numbers' with values one, two, and three. The transition from one-dimensional to two-dimensional lists was explained, highlighting the outer list for rows and inner lists for elements. Additionally, DataFrames can be constructed using dictionaries, where column names are keys and values are lists, illustrated with a 'fruit' and 'price' example. The process of iterating over rows to extract values was also covered, along with creating DataFrames from series, using keys for column names linked to series objects.

children.title: Creating DataFrames with Column Names
children.summary: Different methods for setting and resetting indices in data frames were discussed. The process of creating a data frame using a list with specified column names was explained, emphasizing the importance of naming columns for clarity. An example was provided where a data frame was created with a single column named 'numbers' containing the values one, two, and three, highlighting the structured table format of data frames compared to the series object format.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: There are different ways that we can set index, reset index. So let's move year back to the data frame. If we want to use it later on, and we're going to see that next. you So. The second part or the second way of creating a data frame is using a list with column names if you want to provide column names as well. So for example, if I provide this is very similar to what we had as a series, I would say that okay, I'm creating a data frame object calling the constructor and then I'll say that the values that I want to store is one, two and three. So we did exactly the same thing with series object. This time I'm saying that go ahead and name this column as numbers so that it's not like blank. If you don't name it, it will give you the name zero. So column zero does not have any meaning. You just want to have a description of what attribute you're restoring in that column. So that's why most of the time we would provide a thing for a mission about columns. So I'm saying that columns is equal to numbers and then we move on. So this is a one column data frame, right? You see how it's nicely structured as opposed to if this was a series object, if I was going to print it, it would be like in that veer shape format. But here you're seeing that in kind of a table format. Video: 1: v

leaf_chunk.audio: There are different ways that we can set index, reset index. So let's move year back to the data frame. If we want to use it later on, and we're going to see that next. you So. The second part or the second way of creating a data frame is using a list with column names if you want to provide column names as well. So for example, if I provide this is very similar to what we had as a series, I would say that okay, I'm creating a data frame object calling the constructor and then I'll say that the values that I want to store is one, two and three. So we did exactly the same thing with series object. This time I'm saying that go ahead and name this column as numbers so that it's not like blank. If you don't name it, it will give you the name zero. So column zero does not have any meaning. You just want to have a description of what attribute you're restoring in that column. So that's why most of the time we would provide a thing for a mission about columns. So I'm saying that columns is equal to numbers and then we move on. So this is a one column data frame, right? You see how it's nicely structured as opposed to if this was a series object, if I was going to print it, it would be like in that veer shape format. children.title: Understanding Two-Dimensional Lists in Data Structures
children.summary: To manage multiple columns in data structures, one must transition from a one-dimensional list to a two-dimensional list. The outer list represents different rows, while each inner list corresponds to the elements of those rows. When adding columns, the structure remains two-dimensional, with the inner lists increasing in size to accommodate additional elements. The overall organization involves an outer list containing inner lists, reflecting the tabular arrangement of data.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: What if I have multiple columns? If I have multiple columns, I need to move beyond a one-dimensional list and go into two-dimensional lists. So you think about what the list that we had here, right? So the list that I had here, each this outer list, was basically going over different rows. Each one of those elements, they were referring to different rows that I had. Here is also the same thing. The outer kind of list is going over rows and the inner list, which I have here, is each one of those Actually the... The other way around. So that was the first column and then here I have um each one of those rows. So you're referring to if you're taking a look at one and one, here is one row, and then here this is also row two, and then what you're seeing is here that I'm collecting, all of them stacking them over into that column. And exactly that's what's happening here is that these ones are being stacked. in one column. So that's kind of the structure if you want to keep adding columns. What's happening is that you will still have a 2D structure list. And each one of those inner inner lists are going to be referring to that entire road that you had. So the number of elements that you will put in the inner list is going to increase. But then the structure is still going to be this 2D structure of having one inner list and one outer list as well. So here what I'm saying is that because each one of these in your list have the dimension of two, I have two columns and therefore I'm providing the name for them that the columns are now number. children.title: Creating DataFrames Using Dictionaries
children.summary: DataFrames can be constructed using a dictionary structure where column names serve as keys and their associated values are stored in lists. This method simplifies the organization of data, allowing for easy reference to columns in a key-value format. An example is provided with a DataFrame containing 'fruit' and 'price' as columns, demonstrating how to associate values with these keys. Additionally, the process of iterating over rows to extract specific values for each column is discussed, emphasizing the consistent structure of the DataFrame.

children.title: Creating DataFrames from Series and Dictionaries
children.summary: A data frame can be created using series, where each column name acts as a key in a dictionary, and the corresponding values are series objects. An example is provided where a two-column data frame is constructed with keys 'A' and 'B', each linked to their respective series objects. The values for column B are demonstrated as 'B1', 'B2', and 'B3', with the index labeled as 'R1', 'R2', and 'R3'.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So, the dictionary is out of the way. The second most common way of creating a data frame is using series because naturally we talked about data frame as being a collection of series. Being more accurate by collection, I mean a key value pair. So we have keys being each one of those column names and then values being a series object. So what I can do here is that I can say that, oh, I want a two column data frame. So one column has a name A column, one column has a name B column. So I'm going to go ahead and create a data frame using a dictionary, right? And my dictionary has two columns and two keys. But what are the values? The values that you're seeing here is each one of them is a serious object. So this is a serious object which is created right here, which we know how to create it. Right, and then the values that are associated with B column are also coming from this s underscore B series object. You can see that the values are B1B2B3 and the index is R1R2R3. Video: 1: v

children.title: Creating DataFrames from Series in Pandas
children.summary: To create a DataFrame from multiple Series, they must have matching indices, such as R1, R2, R3. A DataFrame can be viewed as a collection of Series, where each Series represents a key-value pair. Understanding this structure aids in data loading and filtering. Additionally, a Series can be converted into a DataFrame using the DataFrame constructor or by calling the 'series.to_frame()' method.

Audio: The trick here is that if you're Using multiple series in order to combine them in order to create a data frame they need to have matching index and that's why the matching index right here is actually R1 R2R3 they both have R1 R2R3 and that's what's going to show up here R1 R2R3 Cool. children.title: Creating DataFrames from Series Objects
children.summary: A method to convert a Series object into a DataFrame is discussed, highlighting that when columns are not explicitly named, they are automatically labeled starting from zero. This results in the first column being labeled as zero and subsequent columns following suit. The importance of providing column names to avoid default labeling is emphasized.

children.title: Creating and Modifying Non-Numeric Indexes in Pandas
children.summary: The discussion covered creating non-numeric indexes in Pandas, using candidate names from a CSV file as an example. It explained that DataFrames can have non-unique indices, allowing for data retrieval such as counting candidate support. The segment highlighted the use of '.set_index()' to modify indices and '.reset_index()' to revert changes while maintaining column order. It emphasized the importance of unique column names for data integrity, noting that while index values can be non-unique, column names should typically remain unique to prevent confusion. The segment also touched on DataFrame attributes like index, columns, and shape, and the distinction between row and column labels.

children.title: Creating Non-Numeric Indexes in Pandas
children.summary: The discussion focused on the concept of indexes in Pandas, highlighting that indexes can be non-numeric. An example was provided where a CSV file containing election data could have the candidate names set as the index, illustrating how to create a non-numeric index.

children.title: Indexing and Non-unique Indices in DataFrames
children.summary: DataFrames can be indexed to retrieve specific data, such as counting occurrences of a candidate's support. The index in a DataFrame does not have to be unique, as demonstrated by an example where index values are numeric and sequential from zero.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So this is an easy way that if you want to index into a data frame and get all the times that for example is sort of in candidate one friend for presidency and then count how many times it was you can directly use that using this key value kind of structure that we have. One more thing is that the index does not necessarily need to be unique. So in this specific case, in the left-hand side, you're seeing that all of the index values are starting from zero all the way to whatever, and they're numeric, they're unique. Video: 1: v

children.title: Modifying DataFrame Index in Pandas
children.summary: When using a year as an index in a DataFrame, multiple candidates for presidency can exist for the same year, resulting in non-unique index values. This allows for extracting all rows for a specific year. To modify the index, the method '.set_index()' can be used to change which column is indexed. Additionally, the '.reset_index()' method can revert the DataFrame to its original state while maintaining the order of columns. This demonstrates the flexibility of the Index object in a DataFrame.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But in the case that I had year as the index, which we experimented with once, you're seeing that I have multiple different candidates running for presidency in one specific year. for I have non-unique index values. And that's fine. Every time that I index into, like for example, extracting all the rows for 2008, it will give me all of those, right? Although they're not unique. One thing that we can do is we can modify index. So we could say that, okay, you loaded the data frame in a certain way or created the data frame in a certain way. Now I changed my mind. I wanna use my own value. So now I want to move the year or candidate back to the data frame and be able to actually use it and treat it as a feature. We could do that. We could basically say that every time that we want to set the index, we can just call dot set index. We can just provide which column now needs to be indexed. If we go ahead and take a look at the result, you're seeing that now parties, all bolt faces, both face, and then you can kind of see that. It's now indexed. I can go ahead and reset the index. When I say reset index, this means that put part of it back to where it was. It will keep the ordering. It will remember where it was in the sequence of those columns that it had. And then it will automatically create all of these. Automatic in the indices that I had from before, right? So every time you can set it, you can reset it, you can change your mind. There is flexibility into how to use Index object in a data frame. Video: 1: v

leaf_chunk.audio: But in the case that I had year as the index, which we experimented with once, you're seeing that I have multiple different candidates running for presidency in one specific year. for I have non-unique index values. And that's fine. Every time that I index into, like for example, extracting all the rows for 2008, it will give me all of those, right? Although they're not unique. One thing that we can do is we can modify index. So we could say that, okay, you loaded the data frame in a certain way or created the data frame in a certain way. Now I changed my mind. I wanna use my own value. So now I want to move the year or candidate back to the data frame and be able to actually use it and treat it as a feature. We could do that. We could basically say that every time that we want to set the index, we can just call dot set index. We can just provide which column now needs to be indexed. If we go ahead and take a look at the result, you're seeing that now parties, all bolt faces, both face, and then you can kind of see that. It's now indexed. I can go ahead and reset the index. When I say reset index, this means that put part of it back to where it was. It will keep the ordering. It will remember where it was in the sequence of those columns that it had. And then it will automatically create all of these. Automatic in the indices that I had from before, right? So every time you can set it, you can reset it, you can change your mind. There is flexibility into how to use Index object in a data frame.

children.title: Index Values and Column Uniqueness in DataFrames
children.summary: Index values in DataFrames do not need to be unique and can be either numeric or non-numeric. However, column names should typically be unique to avoid confusion, especially when an attribute refers to a candidate name. If there are multiple columns that could represent similar data, discrepancies in values across those columns can lead to problems.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: We said that index values does not need to be unique. They can be numeric They can be non numeric, but for column names, we usually want them to be unique And we just want to make sure that if we have an attribute for candidate or refers to the candidate name We don't have to candidate column and there might be a chance that the values corresponding to a certain row in those different columns are going to be different and that can be problematic, right? children.title: Creating and Accessing DataFrame Indices
children.summary: Unique column names are important for data integrity in a DataFrame. The index can be set to a specific column, such as 'party', which provides an object type index with a length of 182. The DataFrame includes attributes like index, columns, and shape, where 'columns' shows the labels stored in an index object. There are separate indices for row and column labels.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So a lot of times we we want the column names to be unique so that a single source of information is available in each one of the given columns that we have. So, we... I have seen that this index how we can create a data frame, how we can set the index. So here in this case, I created, I set the index to be party. I can go ahead and check the index. The index object for me is now going to be all of these party values that you're seeing. It tells me that the data type is object. The name is party. The length is 182, which we're expecting. I can go ahead and get access to columns. These are attributes that are available on a data frame. Remember that in series object we had index and we had values. So here we have index, we have columns, and then additionally we have shape. So for columns here, it just gives you the labels for the columns. And you're seeing that all of those labels are stored together in an index object. Still we have one index referring to column labels, one index referring to row labels and so on. Video: 1: v

children.title: Understanding DataFrame Dimensions in Pandas
children.summary: The shape of a DataFrame indicates the number of rows and columns, with the first element representing rows and the second representing columns. This information is crucial for assessing the size of the dataset, determining the completeness of the data, and understanding whether the dataset is a full set or just a snapshot.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And then the shape is going to tell you how many rows you have, how many columns you have. The first element is a number of rows. The second element is the number of columns. This is going to be really important if you want to take a look at how many entries you have, how big your data set is and if your data set is complete or is just in a snapshot of a data set that you wish for. So these are the things that you can take a look. Video: 1: v

children.title: Overview of DataFrames and Indexing in Pandas
children.summary: Data frames are collections of series objects sharing a common index, which serves as a unified index for all rows. A data frame is created to explore its structure and columns. The candidate column is set as the index in the selections data frame for better organization, displaying candidate names prominently. The segment discusses resetting the index using 'elections.reset_index()', emphasizing the manipulation of DataFrames. Resetting the index creates a new object unless 'inplace=True' is specified, which modifies the original DataFrame directly; otherwise, changes can be stored in a new DataFrame.

children.title: Data Structures: DataFrames and Series Overview
children.summary: Data frames are collections of series objects that share a common index, which serves as the single index for all rows within the data frame. This structure illustrates how different data structures are interconnected.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Okay, so kind of putting everything together. So this is where done with all the data structures that we wanted to see. We have a data frame that's a collection of series. All of these series objects have a shared index and that shared index is the single index that the data frame has for all of their rows. And this is how all of these treated data structures are connected to each other. So let's take a look at this question. This is the second slide of poll. Video: 1: v

children.title: Resetting Index in Pandas DataFrames
children.summary: Resetting the index in Pandas creates a separate object rather than modifying the original DataFrame unless the parameter 'inplace=True' is specified. This indicates the user's intention to change the original variable directly. Alternatively, changes can be stored in a new DataFrame without altering the original.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I said that... Index to be candidate. I went ahead and said that reset the index. I'm expecting it. The candidate pops back to the normal columns and then I get the automatic index thing, but that didn't happen. Why? It didn't happen is that Pandas has guardrails around making sure that you know what you're doing, right? So when you say that reset index, it will create a separate object. children.title: Creating DataFrames and Using Pandas Resources
children.summary: A DataFrame is created using a dictionary structure with keys as column names (C1 and C2) and values in lists, exemplified by C1 containing 1, 2, 3, 4 and C2 containing 2, 4, 6, 8. The .columns attribute retrieves column labels as an index object. The segment concludes with an overview of DataFrame creation methods in Pandas, including index management, and highlights the extensive API available, noting that not all features will be covered. Students are encouraged to utilize the official pandas documentation for function arguments and examples, a course-provided translation notebook, and to conduct independent research using resources like Stack Overflow to improve their pandas skills for labs and homework.

children.title: Creating DataFrames with Dictionary Structure
children.summary: A DataFrame is created using a dictionary structure where keys represent column names (C1 and C2) and corresponding values are provided in lists. The first column (C1) contains values 1, 2, 3, 4, while the second column (C2) contains values 2, 4, 6, 8. The .columns attribute can be used to retrieve the column labels as an index object, confirming the correct setup of the DataFrame.

children.title: Using Pandas Documentation and Resources
children.summary: The official documentation for pandas is available online, providing detailed descriptions of function arguments and numerous examples. Students are encouraged to explore this documentation, as well as a translation notebook provided in the course. Additionally, they should engage in independent research through resources like Stack Overflow and API documentation to enhance their understanding and proficiency in using pandas for labs and homework.

children.title: Data Extraction Techniques in Pandas
children.summary: Data extraction in Pandas involves selecting specific rows and columns from data frames using label-based methods. Key functions like .head() and .tail() display the first or last five rows, with customizable options. The loc function is crucial for label-based extraction, allowing access to data at specified row and column intersections, accepting lists, slices, or single values. An example illustrates filtering with specific row and column labels, emphasizing the correct format for row labels and noting that column labels are optional. Additionally, it discusses dictionary key errors related to incorrect label indexing and highlights the use of slicing with inclusive bounds for data extraction.

children.title: Data Extraction Techniques and Methods in Pandas
children.summary: Data extraction in Pandas involves selecting specific columns and rows from data frames or series objects, with an emphasis on using labels for this process. Key methods include the .head() and .tail() functions, which display the first or last five rows of a DataFrame by default, although users can specify the number of rows to view. Understanding the dataset's structure through its index and column labels is essential for effective data extraction. Additionally, the loc function allows for label-based extraction, enabling users to access data at the intersection of specified row and column labels.

children.title: Data Extraction Techniques in Pandas
children.summary: Data extraction involves selecting specific columns and rows from a data frame or series object. Common methods include viewing the first or last few rows to understand column labels and data values, which aids in identifying the need for data cleaning. Extraction can be performed using labels or integer positions, emphasizing the importance of labels in the process.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So we're going to talk about how we can do data extraction. So we're going to basically be able to do data extraction in order to select a number of columns, a number of rows, and then be able to just take a look at a snapshot of a data frame or a serious object that you have. There are different ways, different common ways for us to extract the data. There's one way that we can, once I hand you a data, you can take a look at the first few rows or the last few rows. So looking at the top rows or the bottom rows is the usual thing that you would do in order to make sense of, OK, what are the column labels, whether those values looking like, do I need to do any data cleaning or whatnot? We'll keep using it a lot, just to take a look at the top part or the bottom part of the data set. There are different ways to do data extraction using labels. So you might be asking why did I bother about labels a lot because they can be used for data extraction. We might want to also use integer positions in order to figure out how to extract the data. So we're going to basically be able to do data extraction in order to select a number of columns, a number of rows, and then be able to just take a look at a snapshot of a data frame or a serious object that you have. There are different ways, different common ways for us to extract the data. There's one way that we can, once I hand you a data, you can take a look at the first few rows or the last few rows. So looking at the top rows or the bottom rows is the usual thing that you would do in order to make sense of, OK, what are the column labels, whether those values looking like, do I need to do any data cleaning or whatnot? We'll keep using it a lot, just to take a look at the top part or the bottom part of the data set. There are different ways to do data extraction using labels. So you might be asking why did I bother about labels a lot because they can be used for data extraction. We might want to also use integer positions in order to figure out how to extract the data.

children.title: Data Extraction Methods in Pandas
children.summary: The segment discusses methods for extracting data from a DataFrame in Pandas, specifically using the .head() and .tail() functions. These functions allow users to view the top or bottom rows of the DataFrame, respectively. Additionally, an argument can be provided to these functions to specify the number of rows to display.

children.title: Data Extraction in Pandas: Head and Tail Functions
children.summary: The default number of rows displayed when using the head() or tail() functions in Pandas is five. The head() function retrieves the top five rows of a dataset, while the tail() function retrieves the last five rows. It's important to check the dataset's labels, including index and column labels, to understand its structure. Label-based extraction is emphasized as a crucial aspect of working with data in Pandas.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Whether that be the top rows or the bottom rows, it will get you n number of rows. If we don't provide that number, the default value is going to be the default n is equal to five. We're always taking a look at the top five. So for example, in this case, if I have elections, if I go ahead and say dot head five, or just say dot head, it will give me the top five rows. If I take a look at dot tail five or dot tail, it will give me the last five rows. and I should be able to just generally take a look at, okay, does it have labels, what is index, what is column labels, how does the data look like? That's a normal thing that we will always do when we have a new data set in our pile. Using label-based extraction. Remember, I said I have column labels, I have row labels, and by this time you should be tired of me keep repeating that labels are very important. Video: 1: v

children.title: Data Extraction Using loc in Pandas
children.summary: The loc function in Pandas is crucial for locating parts of a data frame using labels. It allows users to extract specific rows and columns by providing row and column labels, enabling access to the data at the intersection of these labels.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: They are very important because we can locate a part of a data frame using those labels. So the function that we'll do that for us is called the lock. This is an operator that uses a score brackets. it will get a number of row labels and it will get a number of column labels and it will be able to extract or give us the numbers those rows or those columns at that specific intersection for us. children.title: Label-based Data Extraction in Pandas
children.summary: This segment covers label-based data extraction using the 'loc' operator in Pandas, which allows users to extract data based on specified row and column labels. It accepts lists, slices, or single values as arguments. An example demonstrates filtering data using specific row labels (87, 25, and 179) and column labels (year, candidate, and result). It highlights the importance of providing row labels in the correct format. Additionally, it discusses that column labels in Pandas are optional; if omitted, all columns are included, and the default behavior filters under rows. A key error may occur if there is a mismatch in ordering or if a single list is incorrectly provided as a column list.

children.title: Label-based Data Extraction with loc
children.summary: The segment discusses label-based extraction using the 'loc' operator in Pandas, explaining that it allows extraction based on specified row and column labels. The operator can accept a list, slice, or single value as arguments. An example is provided where specific row labels (87, 25, and 179) and column labels (year, candidate, and result) are used to filter and obtain the intersection of data. It emphasizes the necessity of always providing row labels in the desired format.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So lock this L refers to label-based extraction. We're doing extraction based on the label. So there are different ways that we can do it. So for example, here the labels, you already know that these are my labels for the rows and the labels for the columns, all of those bold face text that I have. The arguments that can be passed to the lock operator could be a list, could be a slice, or could be a single value. So let's take a look at that together. So if I say that I'm providing a list of labels for the rows. Here's label 87, 25 and 179. It will give me exactly those rows, 87, 25 and 179. Right? And then the list for the columns here, what you're seeing is that I'm asking only for the year column candidate column and result column and I'm only getting those values. So it will basically indexing filtering for the columns, filtering for the rows and only looking at that intersection. Notice that the first argument here is row labels, second argument is column labels. So row labels is always important. You have to always provide row labels in whatever format that you want. children.title: Column Labels and Filtering in Pandas
children.summary: Column labels in Pandas are optional; if omitted, all columns will be included. The default behavior is to filter under rows rather than columns. If there is a mismatch in ordering or if a single list is provided as a column list instead of a rule list, a key error will occur.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But the column labels are optional. If you don't provide column labels, it will give you all of the columns. It mostly assumes that you're filtering under rows, but you're not necessarily filtering under columns. What happens if you have mismatch ordering or if you provide only one list, but that list is your column list. is your column list instead of rule list, it will give you key error. Video: 1: v

children.title: Dictionary Key Errors and Data Extraction in Pandas
children.summary: A key error in a dictionary indicates an attempt to index into a wrong label set. Data extraction in Pandas can be performed using slicing with inclusive upper bounds, starting from the beginning if no lower bound is provided, and going to the end if no upper bound is specified. Specific rows can be extracted while retrieving all columns by specifying desired columns. The segment also covers filtering rows and columns in a DataFrame using the loc and iloc methods, noting that row labels are mandatory while column labels are optional, and the importance of using a colon to indicate all rows to avoid KeyErrors. Extracting a single column from multiple rows results in a Series object. Label-based extraction allows for retrieving specific entries by using row and column labels, with an example involving Andrew Jackson, and mentions that integer-based extraction and various methods will be discussed in the upcoming lecture.

children.title: Dictionary Key Errors and Data Extraction in Pandas
children.summary: A key error in a dictionary indicates an attempt to index into a wrong label set. Data extraction can be done using slicing, where a lower and upper bound can be specified. When extracting columns, it's important to note that the upper bound is inclusive, which is a deviation from typical conventions. If no lower bound is provided, extraction starts from the beginning; if no upper bound is given, it goes to the end. To extract specific rows while retrieving all columns, one can specify the desired columns only. The ellipses in a data frame indicate a summary view, not displaying all data.

children.title: Data Extraction in Pandas with loc and iloc
children.summary: The segment discusses how to filter rows and columns in a Pandas DataFrame using the loc and iloc methods. It emphasizes that while row labels are mandatory, column labels are optional; if not provided, all columns will be returned. The importance of including a colon to indicate all rows is highlighted, as omitting it can lead to a KeyError. Additionally, extracting a single column from multiple rows results in a Series object, which is a filtered version of the original DataFrame.

children.title: Label-based Data Extraction in Pandas
children.summary: Label-based extraction allows for retrieving specific data entries by using row and column labels. For example, accessing the entry for Andrew Jackson involves identifying the first row and the corresponding candidate's column. The upcoming lecture will cover integer-based extraction and various extraction methods.
